questions.stem: Regarding the β-Arrestin pathway in opioid receptors:
questions.explanation:
When a ligand binds to an opioid receptor, it activates two distinct intracellular pathways:

PathwayKey Effects
G-protein (Gᵢ) pathwayInhibition of adenylate cyclase → ↓ cAMP; closure of voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels; opening of voltage-gated K⁺ channels → hyperpolarisation → inhibition of neurotransmitter release
β-Arrestin pathwayReceptor desensitisation and internalisation (contributing to tolerance); side effects including respiratory depression

Biased agonism refers to ligands that preferentially activate one pathway over the other. Biased agonists such as oliceridine favour the G-protein pathway over the β-Arrestin pathway, potentially resulting in less tolerance and fewer side effects (including less respiratory depression).

G-protein signalling cascade — step by step:

  1. Ligand binds to the extracellular N-terminus of the 7-transmembrane Gᵢ-protein coupled receptor
  2. Conformational change → intracellular GDP is exchanged for GTP on the α-subunit
  3. α-GTP dissociates from the βγ dimer
  4. α-GTP inhibits adenylate cyclase → ↓ conversion of ATP to cAMP
  5. βγ dimer closes voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels and opens inwardly-rectifying K⁺ channels
  6. Net result: membrane hyperpolarisation → inhibition of glutamate release and propagation of action potentials in nociceptive fibres

Note: all opioid receptors are 7-transmembrane Gᵢ-protein coupled receptors. The transmembrane domain is highly conserved across receptor subtypes (μ, δ, κ); differences occur mainly in the extracellular regions, which accounts for ligand selectivity.

questions.question: A 60-year-old woman with no significant history presents to clinic complaining of left-sided facial pain. She reports sharp, shooting pains lasting 20 seconds to one minute, triggered by eating, brushing her teeth, talking, and light touch to the side of her face. Between episodes there is no ongoing pain. Clinical examination reveals no abnormality in any territory of the trigeminal nerve. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
A. Incorrect. Cluster headache is a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia (TAC), which is associated with autonomic features (e.g. lacrimation, nasal congestion, ptosis) absent in this case.

B. Incorrect. Trigeminal neuropathic pain presents as a constant pain, and examination will reveal negative and positive neurological signs — the normal examination here makes this diagnosis unlikely.

C. Incorrect. Paroxysmal hemicrania is a TAC, which is also associated with autonomic features absent in this case.

D. Incorrect. TMJ syndrome causes continuous pain that worsens with chewing; examination typically reveals masseter tenderness — neither feature is present here.

E. Correct. Trigeminal neuralgia (TN) presents as short-lasting, lancinating, electric shock-like paroxysms in one or more branches of the trigeminal nerve, triggered by light stimuli (eating, talking, touch). The pain-free interval between attacks and a normal neurological examination are characteristic. First-line treatment is pharmacological (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine). Surgical options — including microvascular decompression — are considered when medical treatment fails, particularly in the presence of a neurovascular conflict on MRI.
